text:
HEMATOLOGY/ONCOLOGY — TREATMENT VISIT NOTE
Midpoint Restaging Review — mFOLFIRINOX Cycle 4 (4 of 8 planned cycles)

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: Medical Oncology
SERVICE: Gastrointestinal Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient — treatment visit with restaging review

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old West African woman with recently diagnosed borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process (KRAS G12V mutant, SMAD4 retained, MSS, TMB-low) presenting today for review of midpoint restaging imaging after completing 4 cycles of neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (mFOLFIRINOX), and for assessment of treatment tolerability, side effect management, and planning for the second half of neoadjuvant therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a retired high school science teacher who initially presented approximately 2 months ago to her primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive epigastric pain radiating to the back, an unintentional weight loss of approximately 14 pounds, and worsening glycemic control despite having had well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin for the prior decade. Cross-sectional imaging with pancreatic protocol CT revealed a 3.4 cm hypoattenuating mass in the pancreatic head/uncinate process with abutment of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) over approximately 180 degrees and narrowing of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) lumen by approximately 40%. PET-CT confirmed FDG-avid disease at the primary site without distant metastases. Endoscopic ultrasound-guided fine needle biopsy confirmed pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated (Grade 2). Molecular profiling (Tempus xT) demonstrated KRAS p.G12V, TP53 p.R248W, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and several additional alterations without actionable targeted therapy implications. Germline testing was negative for pathogenic variants in BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, and mismatch repair genes.

The patient was discussed at our multidisciplinary pancreatic cancer tumor board and the consensus recommendation was neoadjuvant chemotherapy with mFOLFIRINOX — oxali 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, Camptosar 150 mg/m2 (upfront dose reduction from standard 180 mg/m2 given age and diabetic neuropathy risk), and Adrucil 2,400 mg/m2 as a continuous 46-hour infusion, administered every 2 weeks — for a planned 8 cycles, with the intent of tumor downstaging prior to reassessment for chemoradiation and surgical resection. Cycle 1 was initiated without incident. G-CSF (filgrastim) was given prophylactically throughout.

She is now presenting having completed Cycle 4 of the planned 8. She reports gradual improvement in her epigastric and back pain — which has been her most bothersome symptom since diagnosis — describing pain as now a 2/10 on average, down from a 6-7/10 at presentation. Her appetite has improved somewhat, and her weight appears to have stabilized over the last 3-4 weeks. She reports significant nausea as her most troublesome current symptom, which she rates as moderate and partially managed with ondansetron and prochlorperazine. Fatigue has been substantial, interfering with her ability to do her usual daily activities such as gardening and cooking, though she remains ambulatory and self-sufficient. She reports tingling and numbness in her fingertips and toes, worse with cold exposure — consistent with oxali-induced peripheral neuropathy — which she finds bothersome, particularly when handling cold items from the refrigerator. She experienced diarrhea during Cycle 3, which was managed with loperamide and prompted a dose reduction of iri from 150 to 120 mg/m2. No recurrence of grade 2 or higher diarrhea since that adjustment. She has not required any hospitalization or emergency room visits since starting treatment. No febrile episodes. Denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or lower extremity edema. Blood glucose levels have remained elevated, tracking in the 200-260 mg/dL range fasting; she has been working with her endocrinologist and has been started on basal insulin (glargine) in addition to her metformin.

The restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol (performed prior to today's visit) shows the pancreatic head/uncinate mass now measuring 3.0 cm (previously 3.4 cm), with SMA abutment now at approximately 160 degrees (previously greater than 180 degrees) and slightly less pronounced SMV narrowing. No new liver lesions, peritoneal deposits, or lymphadenopathy. CA 19-9 has declined from 1,200 U/mL at baseline to 380 U/mL today — a 68% reduction.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue (grade 2, ongoing). Weight loss stabilized; no further significant weight loss over past 3-4 weeks. Appetite improved compared to presentation.
Neurological: Bilateral fingertip and toe paresthesias (grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy), cold-induced, consistent with oxali effect. No weakness. No ataxia.
Gastrointestinal: Moderate nausea, partially controlled. No vomiting requiring IV hydration. Diarrhea resolved following Camptosar dose reduction. Stool frequency normal. No melena or hematochezia. Mild constipation on some days (antiemetic-related).
Dermatological: Mild hair thinning (grade 1 alopecia). No rash or hand-foot syndrome.
Hematologic: No febrile episodes. No bleeding. No easy bruising beyond expected.
Endocrine/metabolic: Ongoing hyperglycemia, requiring insulin titration. No hypoglycemic episodes.
Musckuloskeletal: Generalized deconditioning. No new bone pain.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or lower extremity swelling.
Pulmonary: No cough, dyspnea, or pleuritic chest pain.
Genitourinary: No dysuria or hematuria.
Psychiatric: Anxiety related to cancer diagnosis and uncertainty about surgical outcome. Patient emotionally engaged and motivated. Support from family members present at visit.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, pancreatic head/uncinate process — clinical stage T3 N0 M0 (Stage IIA at diagnosis); currently on neoadjuvant therapy
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed approximately 10 years prior; worsening glycemic control in the setting of pancreatic cancer and chemotherapy; now on metformin plus basal insulin (glargine)
3. Hypertension — on lisinopril and amlodipine, well-controlled
4. Hyperlipidemia — on atorvastatin, stable
5. No prior surgery
6. No prior malignancy

FAMILY HISTORY

No family history of pancreatic cancer. Mother: deceased, cardiovascular disease. Father: hypertension. One sibling with type 2 diabetes. No known hereditary cancer syndromes in the family. Germline testing performed and negative for BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2 pathogenic variants.

SOCIAL HISTORY

Retired high school science teacher. Lives at home with her husband of 40 years, who accompanies her to most oncology visits and is actively involved in her care. Three adult children, two local. No tobacco use (lifetime nonsmoker). No alcohol use. No illicit drug use. West African descent. Nonsmoker status confirmed. Functional status: ECOG performance status 1 (some limitation in strenuous activity; fully ambulatory and capable of self-care).

ALLERGIES

NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies). No known contrast allergy.

MEDICATIONS

Oncology/active treatment:
1. Eloxatin 85 mg/m2 IV q2 weeks (Cycle 4 of 8)
2. Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV q2 weeks
3. iri 120 mg/m2 IV q2 weeks (dose-reduced from 150 mg/m2 after Cycle 3 diarrhea)
4. 5FU 2,400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours q2 weeks
5. Filgrastim (Neupogen) 480 mcg subcutaneously — days 3-5 per cycle (G-CSF prophylaxis)

Supportive/Symptom management:
6. Ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours as needed for nausea
7. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally every 6 hours as needed for breakthrough nausea
8. Loperamide 2 mg orally as needed for diarrhea (per anti-diarrheal instructions)
9. Lorazepam 0.5 mg orally as needed for anticipatory nausea

Comorbidity management:
10. Metformin 1,000 mg orally twice daily
11. Insulin glargine (Lantus) 18 units subcutaneously at bedtime (recently initiated; dose being titrated)
12. Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily
13. Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily
14. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally at bedtime
15. Pancrelipase (PERT) — not yet initiated (pancreatic exocrine function partially preserved at this time)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg. Heart rate 74 bpm, regular. Respiratory rate 16 breaths/min. Temperature 98.4°F (36.9°C). SpO2 98% on room air. Weight 138 lbs (62.6 kg) — baseline at diagnosis was 152 lbs (69.1 kg); weight loss of approximately 14 lbs since diagnosis, with stabilization over past month. BSA 1.69 m2. ECOG performance status 1.

General: Alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. Mildly fatigued appearance. Cooperative and conversational throughout the visit.

HEENT: Pupils equal, round, and reactive. Sclerae anicteric — no jaundice. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis. Mild hair thinning noted diffusely, consistent with grade 1 alopecia.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No JVD.

Chest/Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema.

Abdomen: Soft and appropriately non-distended. Mild epigastric tenderness to deep palpation — subjectively improved compared to prior visits. No rebound tenderness or guarding. No palpable organomegaly. Port-a-cath site in the right anterior chest wall without signs of infection — no erythema, warmth, or tenderness.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Reduced sensation to light touch in fingertips (bilateral) and toes (bilateral) in a stocking-glove distribution, consistent with grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy. Deep tendon reflexes intact and symmetric. No cerebellar signs. Gait steady.

Skin: No jaundice. No rash. No hand-foot syndrome. Grade 1 alopecia noted on scalp.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No deep venous tenderness.

---

LAB RESULTS

Complete Blood Count (prior to today's Cycle 5 administration):
- WBC: 6.8 x 10^9/L (reference 4.5–11.0)
- ANC: 4.2 x 10^9/L — adequate for chemotherapy administration
- Hemoglobin: 10.6 g/dL (mild anemia; baseline was 12.4 g/dL)
- Hematocrit: 32.1%
- Platelets: 198 x 10^9/L

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.84 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
- BUN: 14 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.9 mg/dL
- Direct bilirubin: 0.3 mg/dL
- Alkaline phosphatase: 98 U/mL (improved from 142 U/mL at diagnosis)
- ALT: 32 U/mL
- AST: 28 U/mL
- Albumin: 3.5 g/dL
- Total protein: 6.8 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 380 U/mL (significantly decreased from 1,200 U/mL at baseline; 68% decline)
- CEA: 3.2 ng/mL (decreased from 5.8 ng/mL at baseline)

Other:
- HbA1c: 8.6% (improved from 9.2% at diagnosis; glycemic control improving with insulin addition)
- Fasting glucose: 214 mg/dL (on morning of visit)
- Magnesium: 1.9 mEq/L (mildly low; supplementation recommended)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Abdomen/Pelvis with Pancreatic Protocol (performed prior to today's visit — midpoint restaging after 4 cycles):
The pancreatic head/uncinate mass has decreased in size from 3.4 cm at baseline to 3.0 cm — a modest but genuine reduction. The degree of SMA abutment has improved, now estimated at approximately 160 degrees of contact compared to greater than 180 degrees at diagnosis. The SMV narrowing has also improved slightly, persisting but appearing somewhat less pronounced. No new hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or lymphadenopathy. The findings are consistent with a minor but meaningful early response to mFOLFIRINOX. The tumor appears somewhat less conspicuous than at baseline, though clearly still present. Overall the imaging is encouraging — the direction of change is exactly what we were hoping to see.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 66-year-old woman with borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head/uncinate process who is now at the midpoint of her planned 8-cycle neoadjuvant mFOLFIRINOX regimen. Overall, the picture at the 4-cycle mark is encouraging:

1. PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA — BORDERLINE RESECTABLE, CURRENTLY ON NEOADJUVANT THERAPY, RESPONDING

The patient has completed 4 of 8 planned cycles of mFOLFIRINOX with a meaningful biochemical response — CA 19-9 has declined from 1,200 U/mL at baseline to 380 U/mL today, a 68% reduction, which is a very encouraging signal. CEA has also declined from 5.8 to 3.2 ng/mL. On imaging, the tumor has decreased from 3.4 cm to 3.0 cm, and the degree of SMA abutment has improved from greater than 180 degrees to approximately 160 degrees. While the tumor remains in contact with the SMA and is not yet clearly resectable, the trajectory is in the right direction. The goal at this juncture is to continue toward the planned 8 total cycles, at which point we will perform definitive restaging CT with pancreatic protocol and MRI liver. Based on response at that time, the multidisciplinary tumor board will re-evaluate the case for further local consolidation with chemoradiation followed by consideration for surgical resection (Whipple procedure).

Plan: Proceed with Cycle 5 of mFOLFIRINOX today. Continue at current doses (Eloxatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, Camptosar 120 mg/m2 — dose-reduced from 150 mg/m2, 5-FU 2,400 mg/m2 46-hour CI). Will continue G-CSF prophylaxis (filgrastim days 3-5). Continue antiemetics as outlined. Plan Cycle 6 in 2 weeks. Definitive restaging imaging to be scheduled after Cycle 8.

2. PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY — GRADE 2 (oxali-ASSOCIATED)

The patient has developed grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy consistent with cumulative Eloxatin exposure. Symptoms include bilateral fingertip and toe paresthesias, worse with cold. At this time, the neuropathy is manageable and she is functional, but we must monitor closely as cumulative dose increases with Cycles 5-8. No dose reduction of Eloxatin is indicated at this time given grade 2 status without functional impairment beyond cold sensitivity. If neuropathy worsens to grade 3 (functional impairment limiting activities of daily living), oxali will need to be held or discontinued with continuation of the remaining agents. Patient counseled extensively regarding cold-avoidance strategies (gloves for refrigerator, avoiding cold liquids, etc.). Prescribed duloxetine 30 mg daily for neuropathic symptom management with plan to titrate to 60 mg if tolerated.

3. NAUSEA AND FATIGUE — GRADE 2

Nausea has been the most bothersome ongoing side effect. Current regimen of ondansetron 8 mg q8h PRN plus prochlorperazine 10 mg q6h PRN for breakthrough is providing partial control. Patient educated on taking ondansetron proactively on the evening before and morning of infusion day. Added olanzapine 2.5 mg orally at bedtime on days 1-4 of each cycle (low-dose olanzapine for chemotherapy-induced nausea, per MASCC/ASCO guidelines), which has good evidence in the setting of multi-day highly emetogenic regimens. Fatigue counseled regarding gentle aerobic activity as tolerated, pacing strategies, and adequate hydration. No anemia-related intervention needed at this time (Hgb 10.6 g/dL, asymptomatic).

4. DIARRHEA — RESOLVED AFTER DOSE REDUCTION

Grade 2 diarrhea in Cycle 3 was managed with loperamide and prompted iri dose reduction to 120 mg/m2. Diarrhea has not recurred at this dose level. Patient to continue current Camptosar dose. She has standing prescription for loperamide and reviewed the anti-diarrheal rescue protocol. She understands to call the oncology triage line promptly if diarrhea worsens to more than 4 stools above baseline per day or if she develops fever in the setting of diarrhea.

5. HYPOMAGNESEMIA — MILD

Magnesium 1.9 mEq/L, mildly low (likely related to 5-FU and oxali). Starting oral magnesium oxide 400 mg twice daily. Will recheck at next visit.

6. DIABETES MELLITUS — WORSENING GLYCEMIC CONTROL IN SETTING OF PANCREATIC CANCER AND CHEMOTHERAPY

HbA1c improved to 8.6% from 9.2% with addition of basal insulin. Fasting glucose remains elevated at 214 mg/dL. Referred back to endocrinology for insulin titration; glargine dose to be increased per endocrinology recommendation. Dexamethasone use has been minimized given diabetic history — antiemetic regimen does not rely on steroids. Continued metformin (renal function adequate).

7. HYPERTENSION AND HYPERLIPIDEMIA — STABLE

Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg on current regimen. No adjustments needed. Atorvastatin and amlodipine/lisinopril continued.

8. NUTRITIONAL STATUS AND WEIGHT

Weight has stabilized, which is a good sign. Patient reports improved appetite compared to presentation. No formal nutrition consultation needed at this time, but dietitian follow-up is available if needed. Ensure adequate protein and caloric intake discussed. Continuing monitoring.

9. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT

Patient and family expressing anxiety about surgical candidacy and the long treatment course. Reassurance provided that the early response signs (CA 19-9 trending down, tumor shrinking, pain improving) are meaningful positive indicators. Social work referral placed for additional psychosocial support resources. Patient aware that final determination of surgical candidacy will be made after restaging and tumor board review following Cycle 8.

---

FOLLOW-UP

- Cycle 6 mFOLFIRINOX in 2 weeks — pre-treatment labs and nursing visit
- Cycle 7 in 4 weeks
- Cycle 8 in 6 weeks, with definitive restaging CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol and MRI liver to be scheduled for approximately 1-2 weeks after Cycle 8
- Following restaging: re-presentation at multidisciplinary GI oncology tumor board
- Phone triage available 24 hours for urgent symptom concerns (fever, severe nausea/vomiting, severe diarrhea)
- CA 19-9 and CEA with each cycle
- Magnesium recheck at next visit

Time spent in face-to-face counseling and coordination of care: 45 minutes.

Electronically signed — Attending Physician, Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology
